descriptions.li: The foul oily smell filling my mouth scrapes sharply at my nerves. I am forcing it down, but I cannot keep my irritation under control.
The taste is truly awful. But if we are going to survive, we have to endure even this filthy oily reek.
The stench of oil is overwhelming. My whole mouth feels like it is burning, but if this is for my survival, it is nothing.
The oily smell is making my stomach turn. It makes my blood boil that I always have to endure something this unpleasant.
Ugh, that disgusting oily smell will not fade. Everyone else is drinking it quietly, so I should endure it too, but it is truly miserable.
What a terrible smell. There is no other choice, so I will drink it, but it feels like my whole body is being caked in grease.
I cannot show weakness in front of my comrades, so I force it down. If we overcome this stench together, that will be enough.
Everyone around me must be enduring this oily smell too. I cannot be the only one making a scene and throwing up, so I grit my teeth and bear it.
If we are not going to fall apart, we have to endure even this rotten oily smell. It is the minimum price for each of us doing our part.
A weakling would have started complaining about this stench already. But my body is mine to look after. I just need to shut up and swallow it.
To think I have to drink this foul oil just to survive. Complaining to others will not change anything, so I have no choice but to swallow it alone.
I hate this terrible smell, but if I collapse, I will be a burden to the whole group. I just want to rinse this filthy taste out of my mouth.
The smell hits like smoke from a bad day. I clench my jaw, swallow the panic, and force my body to obey.
The oily burn drags old fear up from somewhere deep. I go quiet and let it pass over me until my hands stop shaking.
The taste pulls me back to people coughing in the dark. I keep my eyes on the others, because nobody should face that alone.
The reek opens a door I keep shut. I step away inside myself and hold the line from somewhere cold and distant.

li.label: oil smell in my mouth
li.description: I drank naphtha. The oily smell will not leave my mouth.

descriptions.li: The intense oily smell running down my throat slams hard into my brain. This thrilling discomfort is proof that I am alive.
This awful, overpowering scent is filling my whole body with strength. With this momentum, I feel like I could break through any enemy.
What a magnificent stimulus. Others would gag at this smell, but to me it is the finest fuel for waking every sense in my body.
It was foul at first, but this harsh oily smell stirs the venom and catharsis hidden deep in my chest. It is not bad.
Wow, that smell is incredible. Everyone else seems to be suffering, but somehow I like this sharp, stinging sensation.
The acrid oily smell pierces my nose, but quietly savoring this powerful stimulus alone is its own small pleasure.
Can you feel this thrilling rush? This energy is coursing through me so strongly that I want to share it with my comrades.
Why does this oily smell that everyone else avoids excite me so much? I should hide it outwardly, but inside, the electric pleasure will not stop.
I can clearly feel this harsh fuel moving through my body. There may be no better stimulant for keeping us alive.
Anyone too weak to handle this oily smell should step aside. This fierce stimulus that sets my blood boiling is only for the strong.
I do not care what the others think. It is enough that this intense, burning scent is filling my nerves.
Others would call me mad, but the stimulus from this revolting smell is rather excellent. Thanks to it, I can keep moving without tiring for a while.
The rush comes with an old flash of terror. I seize it, twist it into anger, and make it drive me forward.
The fuel steadies me, but the memories underneath it are too loud. I keep my head down and breathe through the shaking.
The burn wakes old fear, and I hate that others might feel it too. I need to make sure everyone stays on their feet.
The relief is real, but so is the old dread behind it. I retreat inward and let the chemistry carry the rest.

li.label: enjoyed raw BTX
li.description: I drank raw BTX compounds. The oily burn was exactly what I wanted.

descriptions.li: The ear-splitting bugle blast forces me forward. I know I have to charge, but this forced slaughter is deeply unpleasant.
The order has come. I have to lead the way for my comrades, but that mad bugle sound is squeezing my heart too tightly.
Charge? Even without that noisy bugle, I would cut down my own enemy. This feeling of being driven toward death is the worst.
I do not want to go. I do not want to die. But that damned bugle is forcing my body to move, and anger boils up inside me.
The bugle is ringing out. Everyone is rushing forward, so I have to go too. Please, let no one die. I am scared.
With that deafening bugle, everyone has started running wild. I am running because I have no choice, but I will look after my own life.
It is the charge order. Follow me. I will not let any of us fall behind. If we face it together, we can break through this terrible pressure.
The bugle shaking the battlefield has left me reeling. I am too scared to move, but I am swept along by my comrades and forced to run.
That is the attack signal. If we want to come back alive, we have no choice but to charge into that hail of bullets. Stay calm and do my part.
That damned bugle is tearing at my eardrums. Do not think you can push me into death. My life is mine to decide. Still, I will tear apart the enemies in front of me.
That noisy bugle is forcing me forward. While the others become bullet catchers, I will find a way to survive on my own.
A forced charge is foolish, but if I do not run to that racket, the comrades beside me will be in danger. I have no choice.
The bugle rips open old battlefield terror. I bare my teeth at it and turn the panic into a command to move.
The bugle makes the past crash over me. I fall silent, follow the line, and try not to let anyone see my hands shake.
The bugle sounds too much like the moments before people die. I lock onto my comrades and run because they need me beside them.
The bugle drags me back somewhere I never wanted to revisit. I move with the charge, but part of me has already gone distant.

li.label: ordered to charge
li.description: The bugle's command is still ringing in my ears, pushing me forward before fear can catch up.

descriptions.li: The officer's saber and neat uniform, poor fits for battle, draw my body upright. They may lack practicality, but they are clearly equipment for honoring someone and preserving dignity.
The shining officer's saber and ordered uniform give mourning a clear form. If the one standing in front does not falter, those behind can lower their heads as well.
This equipment is less for fighting than a sign that the survivors have not become shabby. For the sake of the dead, I do not want to stand here looking disheveled.
It is awkward and cumbersome in combat, but wearing the uniform properly and fastening the officer's saber settles some of the noise inside me. Sometimes this kind of formality is necessary.
The weight of the neat uniform and officer's saber feels unfamiliar and burdensome. Still, this appearance seems suited to a place of mourning, so I quietly straighten my posture.
The gear would get in the way in a real fight, but its neatness lets me hold my feelings at a small distance. Without saying much, this dressed appearance can still convey grief.
Wearing the same formal dress feels like it binds the survivors together. Remembering someone is not only enduring alone; it is also refusing to fall apart together.
I can feel the people around me standing in the same silence. The formality of the officer's saber and uniform speaks grief for us, so no one has to force words out.
The form of mourning protects the order of those left behind. While each of us stands in uniform and in place, it becomes clear that the community has not yet collapsed.
The officer's saber falls short as a battlefield weapon, but that is exactly what gives it meaning now. This is not for fighting, but for respecting the dead in my own way.
It is easier to stand silently in uniform with the officer's saber at my side. I do not have to share grief noisily; I can hold the memory alone in a composed posture.
The neat uniform and ceremonial gear bind personal feelings inside the manners of the community. They create a proper distance: not collapsing completely, but not looking away either.

The collar of the uniform holds my neck upright, and the cold weight of the officer's saber steadies my fingertips. Old gunfire brushes past my ears for a moment, but today it does not pull me along. This formality is not an order that drives fear forward, but a support that lets the living stand straight.

The neatly dressed body remembers its posture before I do. My breathing grows a little shallow, but the lines of the uniform and the weight of the officer's saber slowly gather my scattered thoughts into one place. Today's silence is not a frightening silence, but a quiet space where the heart can settle.

Once I put on the uniform, the empty places become clearer, but strangely that clarity hurts a little less. Remembering those we lost is connected to protecting those still beside us. Because of that, there is a little warmth in the way I check on the comrades around me.

The officer's saber rests at my waist, and the uniform sits without disarray. My emotions take one step back, but this time it does not feel like running away. That cold, orderly distance lets me look at grief slowly, and within it I can find a quiet peace.

li.label: memorial
li.description: I am equipped with the honor guard uniform and officer's saber. They are not practical in combat, but they are enough to honor the dead and preserve the dignity of the living.

li.label: memorial
li.description: I am equipped with the honor guard uniform and officer's saber. They are not practical in combat, but they are enough to honor the dead and preserve the dignity of the living.

li.label: memorial
li.description: I am equipped with the honor guard uniform and officer's saber. They are not practical in combat, but they are enough to honor the dead and preserve the dignity of the living.
